 Divide tmp GaussField by scalar literal with in-place reuse when possible.

 Left division for tmp scalar field (s / tA).

 Right subtraction for tmp scalar field (tA - s).

const label  localFaceI,

)

static scalar Foam::weightedFaceMean ( const faceGaussField< vector > &  faceField,
const label  localFaceI,

)

Compute a quadrature-weighted mean on one vector face trace.

Parameters
faceFieldFace Gauss field

◆ detectScalarField() [1/2]

static bool Foam::detectScalarField ( const dgGeomCell &  cell,
const label  cellID,
const scalar  M,
const bool  includeBoundaryFaces,
const dgField< scalar > &  field,
const GaussField< scalar > &  gaussField 
)
static

Apply the face-by-face TVB-minmod check to one scalar DG field.

Parameters
cellGeometric cell providing size and connectivity
cellIDTarget cell index
MTVB constant in the relaxation threshold
includeBoundaryFacesSwitch to evaluate boundary faces as well
fieldScalar DG field to be checked
gaussFieldCell-local Gauss field for the scalar variable
Returns
True if at least one face violates the TVB-minmod check

◆ detectVectorComponent() [1/2]

static bool Foam::detectVectorComponent ( const dgGeomCell &  cell,
const label  cellID,
const scalar  M,
const bool  includeBoundaryFaces,
const dgField< vector > &  field,
const GaussField< vector > &  gaussField,
const direction  cmpt 
)
static

Apply the face-by-face TVB-minmod check to one vector component.

Parameters
cellGeometric cell providing size and connectivity
cellIDTarget cell index
MTVB constant in the relaxation threshold
includeBoundaryFacesSwitch to evaluate boundary faces as well
fieldVector DG field to be checked
gaussFieldCell-local Gauss field for the vector variable
cmptRequested Cartesian component
Returns
True if at least one face violates the TVB-minmod check

◆ computeHexBasisAndDerivatives()

void Foam::computeHexBasisAndDerivatives ( const vector  etaPt,
const label  pOrder,
List< scalar > &  basis,
List< scalar > &  dBasis_dx,
List< scalar > &  dBasis_dy,
List< scalar > &  dBasis_dz 
)
inline

Evaluate orthonormal modal basis functions for hexahedral cells.

This routine evaluates an L2-orthonormal modal basis defined on the reference hexahedron using tensor products of Legendre polynomials.

The orthonormal basis functions are defined as:

\[ \psi_{i,j,k}(x,y,z) = \sqrt{\frac{(2i+1)(2j+1)(2k+1)}{8}} \; P_i(x)\, P_j(y)\, P_k(z), \qquad i + j + k \le p \]

where:

  • \(P_n\) is the Legendre polynomial of degree \(n\),
  • \((x,y,z) \in [-1,1]^3\) is the reference hexahedral domain,
  • the normalization factor ensures L²-orthonormality:

\[ \int_{[-1,1]^3} \psi_{i,j,k}\,\psi_{m,n,l} \; dx\,dy\,dz = \delta_{im}\,\delta_{jn}\,\delta_{kl}. \]

Therefore, the mass matrix on the reference element is exactly the identity matrix when exact integration is used.

The routine evaluates:

  • the values of all orthonormal modal basis functions at a given point,
  • and their first derivatives with respect to each reference coordinate.
Parameters
etaPtReference coordinates (x,y,z) in [-1,1]^3
pOrderMaximum total polynomial degree
basisValues of the orthonormal basis functions
dBasis_dxDerivatives with respect to x
dBasis_dyDerivatives with respect to y
dBasis_dzDerivatives with respect to z

◆ computePrismBasisAndDerivatives()

void Foam::computePrismBasisAndDerivatives ( const vector  etaPt,
const label  pOrder,
List< scalar > &  basis,
List< scalar > &  dBasis_dx,
List< scalar > &  dBasis_dy,
List< scalar > &  dBasis_dz 
)
inline

Evaluate L2-orthonormal modal basis functions for prism (wedge) cells.

This routine evaluates an L²-orthonormal hierarchical modal basis defined on the reference prism element. The construction follows a Duffy-type tensor-product formulation combining:

  • Legendre polynomials in the η1-direction,
  • collapsed Jacobi polynomials in the (η2,η3) triangular structure.

The orthonormal basis functions are defined as:

\[ \psi_{i,j,k}(\eta_1,\eta_2,\eta_3) = \sqrt{ \frac{ (2i+1)(2j+1)(2k+2j+2) }{8} } \; P_i^{0,0}(\eta_1)\; P_j^{0,0}(\eta_2)\; \left(\frac{1-\eta_3}{2}\right)^j P_k^{2j+1,0}(\eta_3), \]

with index constraints

\[ 0 \le i \le p, \qquad 0 \le j \le p, \qquad 0 \le k \le p-j. \]

where:

  • \(P_n^{\alpha,\beta}\) is the Jacobi polynomial of degree n,
  • \((\eta_1,\eta_2,\eta_3) \in [-1,1]^3\) is the collapsed prism domain,
  • the factor \(\left(\frac{1-\eta_3}{2}\right)^j\) enforces the triangular structure in the (η2,η3) directions,
  • the normalization factor guarantees L²-orthonormality.

The basis satisfies:

\[ \int_{\text{ref prism}} \psi_{i,j,k}\,\psi_{m,n,l} \; d\eta = \delta_{im}\,\delta_{jn}\,\delta_{kl}. \]

Therefore, with exact integration, the reference-element mass matrix is the identity matrix.

Note

Another commonly used orthogonal wedge basis is written in the form:

\[ P_i^{0,0}(\eta_1)\; P_j^{0,0}(\eta_2)\; \left(\frac{1-\eta_3}{2}\right)^j P_k^{2j+1,0}(\eta_3), \qquad i \le p,\; j+k \le p, \]

without explicit normalization.

This alternative formulation appears in the Montjoie finite element framework and related spectral/hp literature.

Reference: https://www.math.u-bordeaux.fr/~durufle/montjoie/wedge.php

A detailed comparison between the present L²-orthonormal scaling and the Montjoie implementation will be investigated in future work.

The routine evaluates:

  • the values of all orthonormal modal basis functions at a point,
  • and their first derivatives with respect to each reference coordinate.
Parameters
etaPtReference coordinates (η1,η2,η3)
pOrderMaximum polynomial order
basisValues of the orthonormal basis functions
dBasis_dxDerivatives with respect to η1
dBasis_dyDerivatives with respect to η2
dBasis_dzDerivatives with respect to η3

This routine evaluates an L²-orthonormal hierarchical modal basis defined on the reference prism element. The construction follows a Duffy-type tensor-product formulation combining:

  • Legendre polynomials in the η1-direction,
  • collapsed Jacobi polynomials in the (η2,η3) triangular structure.

The orthonormal basis functions are defined as:

\[ \psi_{i,j,k}(\eta_1,\eta_2,\eta_3) = \sqrt{ \frac{ (2i+1)(2j+1)(2k+2j+2) }{8} } \; P_i^{0,0}(\eta_1)\; P_j^{0,0}(\eta_2)\; \left(\frac{1-\eta_3}{2}\right)^j P_k^{2j+1,0}(\eta_3), \]

with index constraints

\[ 0 \le i \le p, \qquad 0 \le j \le p, \qquad 0 \le k \le p-j. \]

where:

  • \(P_n^{\alpha,\beta}\) is the Jacobi polynomial of degree n,
  • \((\eta_1,\eta_2,\eta_3) \in [-1,1]^3\) is the collapsed prism domain,
  • the factor \(\left(\frac{1-\eta_3}{2}\right)^j\) enforces the triangular structure in the (η2,η3) directions,
  • the normalization factor guarantees L²-orthonormality.

The basis satisfies:

\[ \int_{\text{ref prism}} \psi_{i,j,k}\,\psi_{m,n,l} \; d\eta = \delta_{im}\,\delta_{jn}\,\delta_{kl}. \]

Therefore, with exact integration, the reference-element mass matrix is the identity matrix.

Note

Another commonly used orthogonal wedge basis is written in the form:

\[ P_i^{0,0}(\eta_1)\; P_j^{0,0}(\eta_2)\; \left(\frac{1-\eta_3}{2}\right)^j P_k^{2j+1,0}(\eta_3), \qquad i \le p,\; j+k \le p, \]

without explicit normalization.

This alternative formulation appears in the Montjoie finite element framework and related spectral/hp literature.

Reference: https://www.math.u-bordeaux.fr/~durufle/montjoie/wedge.php

A detailed comparison between the present L²-orthonormal scaling and the Montjoie implementation will be investigated in future work.

The routine evaluates:

  • the values of all orthonormal modal basis functions at a point,
  • and their first derivatives with respect to each reference coordinate.
Parameters
etaPtReference coordinates (η1,η2,η3)
pOrderMaximum polynomial order
basisValues of the orthonormal basis functions
dBasis_dxDerivatives with respect to η1
dBasis_dyDerivatives with respect to η2
dBasis_dzDerivatives with respect to η3

◆ computePyramidBasisAndDerivatives()

void Foam::computePyramidBasisAndDerivatives ( const vector  etaPt,
const label  pOrder,
List< scalar > &  basis,
List< scalar > &  dBasis_deta1,
List< scalar > &  dBasis_deta2,
List< scalar > &  dBasis_deta3 
)
inline

Evaluate L2-orthonormal modal basis functions for pyramid cells.

This routine evaluates an L²-orthonormal hierarchical modal basis defined on the reference pyramid element. The construction follows the standard collapsed-coordinate formulation using Jacobi polynomials to ensure regular behaviour at the apex.

The orthonormal basis functions are defined as:

\[ \psi_{i,j,k}(\eta_1,\eta_2,\eta_3) = \sqrt{ \frac{ (2i+1)(2j+1)(2k+2(i+j)+3) }{8} } \; P_i^{0,0}(\eta_1)\, P_j^{0,0}(\eta_2)\, \left(\frac{1-\eta_3}{2}\right)^{i+j}\, P_k^{2(i+j)+2,\,0}(\eta_3), \]

with index ranges

\[ 0 \le i \le p, \qquad 0 \le j \le p-i, \qquad 0 \le k \le p-i-j. \]

where:

  • \(P_n^{\alpha,\beta}\) denotes the Jacobi polynomial,
  • \((\eta_1,\eta_2,\eta_3) \in [-1,1]^3\) is the collapsed cube domain,
  • the factor \(\left(\frac{1-\eta_3}{2}\right)^{i+j}\) enforces the pyramid collapse and guarantees regularity at the apex,
  • the normalization factor guarantees L²-orthonormality.

The basis satisfies:

\[ \int_{\text{ref pyramid}} \psi_{i,j,k}\,\psi_{m,n,l} \; d\eta = \delta_{im}\,\delta_{jn}\,\delta_{kl}. \]

Therefore, with exact integration, the reference-element mass matrix is the identity matrix.

Note

Another commonly used orthogonal pyramid basis is written as:

\[ P_i^{0,0}(\eta_1)\, P_j^{0,0}(\eta_2)\, \left(\frac{1-\eta_3}{2}\right)^{\max(i,j)}\, P_k^{2\max(i,j)+2,\,0}(\eta_3), \]

with index constraints

\[ i \le p,\quad j \le p,\quad k \le p-j. \]

This formulation appears in the Montjoie finite element framework and related spectral/hp literature.

Reference: https://www.math.u-bordeaux.fr/~durufle/montjoie/pyramid.php

A detailed comparison between the present L²-orthonormal scaling and the Montjoie implementation will be investigated in future work.

The routine evaluates:

  • the values of all orthonormal modal basis functions at a point,
  • and their first derivatives with respect to each reference coordinate.
Parameters
etaPtReference coordinates (η1,η2,η3)
pOrderMaximum polynomial order
basisValues of the orthonormal basis functions
dBasis_deta1Derivatives with respect to η1
dBasis_deta2Derivatives with respect to η2
dBasis_deta3Derivatives with respect to η3

This routine evaluates an L²-orthonormal hierarchical modal basis defined on the reference pyramid element. The construction follows the standard collapsed-coordinate formulation using Jacobi polynomials to ensure regular behaviour at the apex.

The orthonormal basis functions are defined as:

\[ \psi_{i,j,k}(\eta_1,\eta_2,\eta_3) = \sqrt{ \frac{ (2i+1)(2j+1)(2k+2(i+j)+3) }{8} } \; P_i^{0,0}(\eta_1)\, P_j^{0,0}(\eta_2)\, \left(\frac{1-\eta_3}{2}\right)^{i+j}\, P_k^{2(i+j)+2,\,0}(\eta_3), \]

with index ranges

\[ 0 \le i \le p, \qquad 0 \le j \le p-i, \qquad 0 \le k \le p-i-j. \]

where:

  • \(P_n^{\alpha,\beta}\) denotes the Jacobi polynomial,
  • \((\eta_1,\eta_2,\eta_3) \in [-1,1]^3\) is the collapsed cube domain,
  • the factor \(\left(\frac{1-\eta_3}{2}\right)^{i+j}\) enforces the pyramid collapse and guarantees regularity at the apex,
  • the normalization factor guarantees L²-orthonormality.

The basis satisfies:

\[ \int_{\text{ref pyramid}} \psi_{i,j,k}\,\psi_{m,n,l} \; d\eta = \delta_{im}\,\delta_{jn}\,\delta_{kl}. \]

Therefore, with exact integration, the reference-element mass matrix is the identity matrix.

Note

Another commonly used orthogonal pyramid basis is written as:

\[ P_i^{0,0}(\eta_1)\, P_j^{0,0}(\eta_2)\, \left(\frac{1-\eta_3}{2}\right)^{\max(i,j)}\, P_k^{2\max(i,j)+2,\,0}(\eta_3), \]

with index constraints

\[ i \le p,\quad j \le p,\quad k \le p-j. \]

This formulation appears in the Montjoie finite element framework and related spectral/hp literature.

Reference: https://www.math.u-bordeaux.fr/~durufle/montjoie/pyramid.php

A detailed comparison between the present L²-orthonormal scaling and the Montjoie implementation will be investigated in future work.

The routine evaluates:

  • the values of all orthonormal modal basis functions at a point,
  • and their first derivatives with respect to each reference coordinate.
Parameters
etaPtReference coordinates (η1,η2,η3)
pOrderMaximum polynomial order
basisValues of the orthonormal basis functions
dBasis_deta1Derivatives with respect to η1
dBasis_deta2Derivatives with respect to η2
dBasis_deta3Derivatives with respect to η3

◆ computeTetBasisAndDerivatives()

void Foam::computeTetBasisAndDerivatives ( const vector  etaPt,
const label  pOrder,
List< scalar > &  basis,
List< scalar > &  dBasis_deta1,
List< scalar > &  dBasis_deta2,
List< scalar > &  dBasis_deta3 
)
inline

Evaluate L2-orthonormal modal basis functions for tetrahedral cells.

This routine evaluates an L²-orthonormal hierarchical modal basis defined on the reference tetrahedron. The polynomial space coincides with the classical tetrahedral finite element space of total degree less than or equal to p, but the basis is constructed to be orthonormal with respect to the L² inner product.

The orthonormal basis functions are defined as:

\[ \psi_{i,j,k}(\eta_1,\eta_2,\eta_3) = \sqrt{ \frac{ (2i+1) (2j+2i+2) (2k+2i+2j+3) }{8} } \; P_i^{0,0}(\eta_1) \left(\frac{1-\eta_2}{2}\right)^i P_j^{2i+1,0}(\eta_2) \left(\frac{1-\eta_3}{2}\right)^{i+j} P_k^{2i+2j+2,0}(\eta_3), \qquad i + j + k \le p, \]

where:

  • \(P_n^{\alpha,\beta}\) denotes the Jacobi polynomial,
  • \((\eta_1,\eta_2,\eta_3) \in [-1,1]^3\) is the collapsed cube domain,
  • the factors \(\left(\frac{1-\eta_2}{2}\right)^i\) and \(\left(\frac{1-\eta_3}{2}\right)^{i+j}\) induce the tetrahedral collapse structure,
  • the normalization factor guarantees L²-orthonormality.

The basis satisfies:

\[ \int_{\text{ref tetra}} \psi_{i,j,k}\,\psi_{m,n,l} \; d\eta = \delta_{im}\,\delta_{jn}\,\delta_{kl}. \]

Therefore, with exact integration, the reference-element mass matrix is the identity matrix.

The routine evaluates:

  • the values of all orthonormal modal basis functions at a point,
  • and their first derivatives with respect to each reference coordinate.
Parameters
etaPtReference coordinates (η1,η2,η3)
pOrderMaximum polynomial order
basisValues of the orthonormal basis functions
dBasis_deta1Derivatives with respect to η1
dBasis_deta2Derivatives with respect to η2
dBasis_deta3Derivatives with respect to η3

◆ squareToTriangle()

vector2D Foam::squareToTriangle ( const vector2D &  etaPt)
inline

Map a point from reference square [-1,1]^2 to reference triangle.

Applies the Duffy transformation to collapse one edge of the square into the triangle apex: a = (1 + xi)/2 b = (1 + eta)/2 r = a s = b*(1 - a)

Parameters
xiFirst square coordinate
etaSecond square coordinate
Returns
Triangle coordinates (r, s)

)

Mapping a point from reference space to physical space for an hexahedral element.

◆ mapXToEtaHex()

vector Foam::mapXToEtaHex ( const vector &  x,
const List< vector > &  cellVertices 
)
inline

Map a physical point to reference coordinates of a trilinear hex.

Solves x = sum_i N_i(eta) Xi using Newton iteration. The mapping is trilinear, therefore non-affine.

Parameters
xPhysical point
cellVerticesList of 8 hex vertices [A,B,C,D,E,F,G,H]
Returns
Reference coordinates (eta1, eta2, eta3)

const List< vector > &  cellVertices 
)

Parameters
etaReference coordinates (eta1, eta2, eta3)
cellVerticesPhysical coordinates of the prism vertices (ordered as E, A, B, F, D, C)

◆ mapXToEtaPrism()

Foam::vector Foam::mapXToEtaPrism ( const vector &  x,
const List< vector > &  cellVertices 
)
inline

Map a physical point to reference coordinates of a 6-node prism.

Solves x = sum_i N_i(eta) Xi using Newton iteration. The mapping is trilinear and non-affine.

Parameters
xPhysical point
cellVerticesList of 6 prism vertices [E,A,B,F,D,C]
Returns
Reference coordinates (eta1, eta2, eta3)

◆ mapEtaToXTet()

vector Foam::mapEtaToXTet ( const vector &  eta,
const List< vector > &  cellVertices 
)
inline

Mapping a point from reference space (eta) to physical space (x) for a tetrahedral element.

Parameters
etaReference coordinates (eta1, eta2, eta3)
cellVerticesPhysical coordinates of the tetrahedron vertices (ordered as A, B, C, D)

◆ mapXToEtaTet()

vector Foam::mapXToEtaTet ( const vector &  x,
const List< vector > &  cellVertices 
)
inline

Map a physical point to collapsed reference coordinates of a tetrahedron.

Computes barycentric coordinates by solving the affine mapping:

x = A + λB (B-A) + λC (C-A) + λD (D-A)

Then converts barycentric values to collapsed coordinates:

eta3 = 2*λD - 1 eta2 = 2*λC/(1-λD) - 1 eta1 = 2*λB/(1-λC-λD) - 1

Parameters
xPhysical point
cellVerticesList of 4 tetra vertices [A,B,C,D]
Returns
Reference coordinates (eta1, eta2, eta3)

◆ mapEtaToXPyramid()

vector Foam::mapEtaToXPyramid ( const vector &  eta,
const List< vector > &  cellVertices 
)
inline

Mapping a point from reference space (eta) to physical space (x) for a pyramid element.

Parameters
etaReference coordinates (eta1, eta2, eta3)
cellVerticesPhysical coordinates of the pyramid vertices (ordered as A, B, C, D, E)

◆ mapXToEtaPyramid()

Foam::vector Foam::mapXToEtaPyramid ( const vector &  x,
const List< vector > &  cellVertices 
)
inline

Map a physical point to reference coordinates of a 5-node pyramid.

Solves x = sum_i N_i(eta) Xi using Newton iteration. The mapping is trilinear and non-affine.

Parameters
xPhysical point
cellVerticesList of 5 pyramid vertices [A,B,C,D,E]
Returns
Reference coordinates (eta1, eta2, eta3)

◆ mapToPhysicalTriangle()

Foam::vector Foam::mapToPhysicalTriangle ( const vector2D &  rs,
const List< vector > &  vertices 
)
inline

Map reference triangle coordinates to physical space.

Computes the physical point X from barycentric coordinates (r, s) on the reference triangle using:

X = (1 - r - s) P0 + r P1 + s P2

Parameters
rsReference coordinates (r, s) in the reference triangle
verticesPhysical coordinates of the triangle vertices (ordered as A, B, C)
Returns
Physical 3D coordinate

◆ mapToPhysicalQuad()

Foam::vector Foam::mapToPhysicalQuad ( const vector2D &  eta,
const List< vector > &  vertices 
)
inline

Map reference square coordinates to physical quadrilateral.

Uses bilinear shape functions for a 4-node quad.

Parameters
xiEtaReference coordinates (xi, eta) in [-1,1]^2
verticesPhysical coordinates of the quadrilateral vertices (ordered as A, B, C, D)
Returns
Physical 3D coordinate
